question: What is the fundamental assumption that similarity-based learning methods rely on, and why is the choice of distance metric so crucial?
expectedPoints: The core assumption is that similar inputs (based on their feature values) will have similar outputs or belong to the same class.
The distance metric (e.g., Euclidean, Manhattan, Cosine) quantitatively defines what 'similar' means in the feature space.
A poor choice of metric can distort the true notion of similarity, leading to inaccurate predictions and poor model performance.

question: Describe a key advantage and a key disadvantage of the k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) algorithm.
expectedPoints: Advantage: It is a lazy learner, meaning it makes no assumptions about the underlying data distribution, making it very flexible and simple to implement.
Disadvantage: It is computationally expensive at prediction time because it requires storing the entire training dataset and calculating distances to all instances for each new query.

question: Compare and contrast the k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN) algorithm with a typical parametric model like Logistic Regression. Discuss their approach to learning, model complexity, interpretability, and performance with large datasets.
expectedPoints: Learning Approach: k-NN is an instance-based/lazy learner that memorizes training data; Logistic Regression is a model-based/eager learner that derives a parameterized function.
Model Complexity: k-NN's complexity is determined by the data (number of instances); Logistic Regression's complexity is fixed by the number of features.
Interpretability: The parameters of Logistic Regression are highly interpretable; the predictions of k-NN are less intuitive and based on local neighbors.
Large Datasets: k-NN becomes computationally prohibitive; Logistic Regression is efficient to train and predict with once the parameters are learned.
